_meta.project: ALFAC — Aquatic Literacy For All Children
_meta.note_v2: Fields session_phase/intensity/prerequisites/material_required/substitutes/family_label/family_prerequisites/difficulty_stars are editorial additions for the Toolkit PWA — not present on alfac.eu v2.1 adds goal/success_criteria/setup/group_management/description_full/variations from detailed scraping.

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: Children take turns being the 'siren' who calls others underwater. The siren submerges and makes sounds or signals; the others must dive to hear or see the signal.
description.fr: Les enfants se relaient pour jouer la 'sirène' qui appelle les autres sous l'eau. La sirène plonge et émet des sons ou des signaux ; les autres doivent plonger pour entendre ou voir le signal.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can put their face in the water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut mettre le visage dans l'eau

tasks.goal:
I CAN control exhale while submerged.
I KNOW that sound spreads underwater so that I can hear my friend's voice and that when I exhale air under the water, the water does not come into my mouth.
I FEEL that the appropriate exhalation force will remove water from my mouth. Ok with some water in my mouth
tasks.success_criteria:
Basic and advanced instruction: Giving the correct number said underwater.\r\n
Lower level instruction: Shouting and submersion while screaming continuously without closing the mouth, with open eyes, no coughing.
tasks.setup:
Shallow water, swimming pool, clear and calm open water.\r\n
No additional equipment is needed (Some weight can be used to make the submerged more easily).
tasks.group_management:
Basic and advanced instruction: The task is performed in pairs\r\n
Lower level instruction: The task is performed by the whole group simultaneously.\r\n
Instructor outside the pool.\r\n
Safety guaranteed by lifeguard.

description_full.en: Basis Aquatic Skills: full immersion, holding breath underwater, exhaling underwater.

variations.easier: The Siren stays at the surface and dips only their face, making the signal at the waterline.
variations.harder: The Siren must hold the signal for 3 seconds and move laterally while submerged.

tasks.progression:
Basic instruction: Children stay close to each other in the water. At the instructor's signal, they simultaneously dive underwater and one of the co-exercisers says a number of their choice from 10 to 100 underwater. The other's task is to stay underwater and try to hear and recognize the number. The children come to the surface and the listener says the number he or she has heard. So children can check the performance of the exercise. The task is repeated by each participant three times.\r\n

Advanced instruction: the children's task is to identify a fragment of a song together, e.g. a short verse, and then try to sing it underwater.
tasks.author_text: Team POL, Marek Rejman, Anna Kwasna & Daria Rudnik

description.en: Children dive to retrieve toys or objects from the pool floor. Gradually increase depth as confidence grows.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can put their face in water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut mettre le visage dans l'eau

tasks.goal: I EXPLORE the underwater environment with confidence
tasks.success_criteria: The child dives to retrieve at least one object from the pool floor without assistance.
tasks.setup: Pool (shallow recommended for beginners, deep for advanced). Diving rings or waterproof toys placed on the pool floor.
tasks.group_management: Individual or pairs. Each child has a designated zone. Teacher counts retrieved objects.

description_full.en:
Rings or coloured toys are scattered on the pool floor. At the signal, children dive to collect as many objects as possible within the time limit (30-60 seconds). Objects are placed at increasing depths as confidence grows.

Phase 1: Objects placed in shallow water (50-80 cm). Phase 2: Objects at full pool depth. Phase 3: Objects at 1.2-1.5 m, requiring a full dive. Phase 4: Timed competition.

variations.easier: Place objects at arm's reach depth; child only needs to dip face to reach them.
variations.harder: Objects are placed under a floating mat - child must submerge AND navigate under the obstacle.

description.en: The teacher or a partner provides light physical support under the child's back while the child practices floating. Support is gradually reduced as confidence grows.
description.fr: L'enseignant ou un partenaire apporte un léger soutien physique sous le dos de l'enfant pendant qu'il s'entraîne à flotter. Le soutien est progressivement réduit.
description.no: Læreren eller en partner gir lett fysisk støtte under barnets rygg mens barnet øver på å flyte.

safety_notes.en: Ensure shallow water for beginners. Never leave the child unsupported without warning.
safety_notes.fr: Veiller à l'eau peu profonde pour les débutants.

family_prerequisites.en: No prior experience needed
family_prerequisites.fr: Aucune expérience requise

tasks.goal: I DISCOVER the sensation of floating
tasks.success_criteria: The child maintains a horizontal floating position for at least 5 seconds with minimal support.
tasks.setup: Shallow pool (max 1.2 m). No equipment required. Teacher or partner is in the water.
tasks.group_management: Pairs or individual with teacher support. Partner places hands lightly under the floating child's back.

description_full.en:
The child lies back in the water while the teacher or partner places both hands flat under their upper back. The child is encouraged to look up at the ceiling, let their ears go underwater, and spread their arms wide. The supporter gradually reduces contact.

Key cues: Let your tummy float up to the sky, Make your body like a starfish, Your ears can hear the water - that is OK.

Progression: (1) Both hands supported -> (2) Fingertips only -> (3) One hand removed -> (4) Partner hovers hands 2 cm below without touching -> (5) Independent float for 5 seconds.

variations.easier: Begin with the child holding the pool wall with both hands, feet floating up, head back.
variations.harder: Child floats independently and attempts to close eyes.

description.en: Children line up along the pool edge and jump in one after another in domino fashion, creating a chain of splashes.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can enter the water safely
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut entrer dans l'eau en sécurité

tasks.goal: I ENTER the water joyfully and with confidence
tasks.success_criteria: Each child jumps in at the moment the previous child surfaces, maintaining the chain without breaking.
tasks.setup: Deep pool (minimum 1.5 m). Pool edge. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Groups of 6-10. Children line up along the pool edge with 1 m spacing.

description_full.en:
Children stand in a line along the pool edge, each spaced 1 m apart. The first child jumps in. As soon as they surface and raise one arm (the signal), the next child jumps. The chain continues to the last child.

Progression: (1) Children jump feet-first from standing -> (2) Children try synchronised jumps in pairs -> (3) Introduce a spin or tuck before entry -> (4) Full group synchronised jump at teacher signal.

variations.easier: Children enter by sitting on the edge and sliding in, triggering the next child.
variations.harder: Each child must call out the next child name before jumping.

description.en: Children move under water like a submarine, exploring how to control their body to sink and rise, experimenting with breathing and buoyancy.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can put their face in water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut mettre le visage dans l'eau

tasks.goal: I EXPLORE buoyancy and underwater movement
tasks.success_criteria: The child can submerge fully, move horizontally underwater for at least 3 metres, and surface in a controlled manner.
tasks.setup: Shallow pool (0.8-1.2 m). No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Individual exploration, then paired observation. Partners take turns being the submarine and watching.

description_full.en:
The child is invited to become a submarine. They experiment with: sinking (exhaling to go down), surfacing (inhaling, pushing arms down), moving forward underwater, periscope mode (only eyes and nose above water), full dive and glide.

The teacher uses submarine vocabulary: Dive!, Surface!, Periscope up!, All stop!

Key questions: How do you make yourself go down? What happens when you blow bubbles? Can you move without your arms?

Progression: (1) Explore sinking and surfacing on the spot -> (2) Submerge and glide 2 m -> (3) Navigate between two markers -> (4) Surface at a predetermined target point.

variations.easier: Child begins in shallow water where they can touch the bottom.
variations.harder: Child must complete a figure-8 course underwater using only legs, arms along sides.

description.en: Children practice the insweep arm movement (sculling) to generate propulsion on their belly, focusing on hand and wrist motion.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float on their belly
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter sur le ventre

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP arm propulsion technique
tasks.success_criteria: The child can move forward using sculling arm movement without kicking, maintaining a horizontal position.
tasks.setup: Pool (any depth). Lane ropes for support if needed.
tasks.group_management: Individual. Children work in their own lane. Teacher observes hand position.

description_full.en:
The child lies prone (belly down) and uses the sculling insweep action: hands angled outward on the outsweep, then angled inward with wrist flexed on the insweep, creating a figure-8 path.

Key coaching points: hands always move through water, elbows stay slightly bent, the push comes from the inward sweep.

Progression: (1) Child holds wall and practices arm action only -> (2) Float with kickboard between legs -> (3) Full sculling without support -> (4) Sculling with eyes above water -> (5) Timed 10 m sculling race.

variations.easier: Practice the hand motion sitting on the pool edge first.
variations.harder: Scull on the back (supine position).

description.en: Children alternate between different leg kick styles (crawl, breaststroke, dolphin kick) to discover which generates the most speed and comfort.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can move forward in water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut avancer dans l'eau

tasks.goal: I EXPERIMENT with different leg kick styles
tasks.success_criteria: The child can demonstrate at least two distinct kick styles and describe which feels most effective.
tasks.setup: Pool (any depth). Kickboards required.
tasks.group_management: Individual. Each child has a kickboard. Teacher calls the kick style to switch.

description_full.en:
Children hold a kickboard and travel the length of the pool, switching kick style on the teacher signal. Styles: (1) Flutter kick, (2) Breaststroke kick, (3) Dolphin kick, (4) Bicycle kick.

After trying each style for one length, children vote on which felt fastest, most tiring, most natural.

Discussion: Why do different kicks work better for different swimmers? Which kick works underwater?

Progression: (1) Explore freely -> (2) Time each style over 25 m -> (3) Mix kicks within one length on cue -> (4) Drop the kickboard and attempt each kick style unsupported.

variations.easier: Children hold the wall and practice leg action before using the kickboard.
variations.harder: Attempt the same experiment underwater - which kick is most efficient submerged?

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: Children use a kickboard or noodle as a 'surfboard' and try to glide and steer across the pool, experimenting with body position and arm movements.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can move forward in water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut avancer dans l'eau

tasks.goal:
I KNOW how to stay on the board in a vertical position.
I CAN make hand movements as to slalom while standing on the board in the water
tasks.success_criteria: The participant who completes the 10m slalom in the shortest time
tasks.setup:
You need swimming boards and any 10 accessories scattered over the surface of the water.
Deep water, swimming pool.
tasks.group_management: Task performed individually on time.

description_full.en: Participants must be comfortable in deep water.

variations.easier: Child holds the kickboard under their chest while kicking.
variations.harder: Two children share one kickboard and must coordinate to move in a straight line.

tasks.progression:
Instruction: Participants stand vertically on the boards with their legs flexed at the knees, submerged in water. They make movements with their hands so that they can (without falling off the board) slalom through the water without touching among the objects scattered, floating on the water.\r\n
The swimming course is arbitrary, but a distance of 10m must be covered in the shortest possible time. Any 10 objects float freely along the route.\r\n
Every time a participant touches an object during the slalom, an additional 1s penalty is added.\r\n

Lower level instruction : Participants perform the same task, but in a seated position on a board.
tasks.author_text: Team Poland, Marek, Anna and Daria

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: Children must cross the pool from one floating mat to another without touching the water surface — simulating crossing a river on floating stones. Encourages decision-making and exit skills.
description.fr: Les enfants doivent traverser le bassin d'un tapis flottant à l'autre sans toucher la surface de l'eau.

safety_notes.en: Deep water only with children who have achieved at least Entry level 3.
safety_notes.fr: Eau profonde uniquement avec des enfants ayant atteint au moins le niveau 3 d'entrée.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim forward and exit the water alone
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager et sortir seul de l'eau

tasks.goal: I CAN jump onto a floating mat from the pool edge, move across mats of various sizes and buoyancies, and cover a specified distance to the opposite edge without falling into the water.
tasks.success_criteria: Cross by entering the water and exiting safely while remaining calm.
tasks.setup: Place large floating mats across the width of the pool, along the edge. Space them one meter apart.
tasks.group_management: The activity is done in a group, but participants perform the task individually.

description_full.en: Risk assessment during the jump onto the mat from the pool edge and when jumping from mat to mat. Swimming skills are not essential when distances between the mats are short.

variations.easier: Use a pool noodle as a bridge between the mats.
variations.harder: Timed challenge - beat your previous crossing time, or complete the crossing while holding a floating object.

tasks.progression: Instructions: “Cross the river by jumping into the water, climbing onto the mat, and continuing until you reach the other side. Be careful, when jumping, I don't hold on and jump into the water, not onto the next mat.”\r\n
tasks.author_text: Team Poland, Marek, Anna and Daria

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: A tag game played mostly underwater: one child is the 'shark', others are 'fish'. Fish can only be safe when fully submerged. Encourages voluntary submersion.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim underwater briefly
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager brièvement sous l'eau

tasks.goal:
I CAN dive and perform the task at the bottom of the pool, using both underwater orientation and visual information.
I KNOW how to assess the depth to which I can dive.
I FEEL ready to assess the probability of successfully diving to a given depth.
tasks.success_criteria: Help as many fish as possible to return to houses of the same colour by diving only twice.
tasks.setup:
Swimming pool or open water (swim buoy for safety) with different depths available.
Sinking toys to make pairs of the same colors, eg. fishes and circles.
tasks.group_management:
The task is performed in a group. Instructor outside or inside the pool.
For safety reasons, direct supervision by a lifeguard is required.

description_full.en: Submerge the head.

variations.easier: Safety zone: fish can stand on the pool floor - only partial submersion required.
variations.harder: Fish must touch a specific wall while avoiding sharks.

tasks.progression:
The child's task is to help as many fish as possible return home. Pairs of coloured circles (houses) are located at the bottom of a pool of varying depths, with each circle corresponding to a fish.\r\n
The equipment is located in four areas at different depths. The deeper the water, the more pairs there are to match.\r\n
Children can only dive twice, so they must decide which two fish colonies to choose based on their knowledge of their diving skills.\r\n
Should they take a risk by choosing the larger group to earn a high score in one go, even though they will have to dive deeper in one breath? Or should they choose the smaller group and help fewer fish?
tasks.author_text: Team Poland, Marek, Anna and Daria

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: Children practice the flutter kick for front crawl using a kickboard, focusing on keeping legs straight with small fast kicks from the hip.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float on their belly with support
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter sur le ventre avec aide

tasks.goal: I PRACTICE the flutter kick for front crawl
tasks.success_criteria: The child can travel 10 m using only flutter kick with a kickboard, maintaining straight direction.
tasks.setup: Pool (any depth). Kickboards required. Lanes preferred.
tasks.group_management: Individual in lanes. Teacher observes from poolside.

description_full.en:
Children hold a kickboard at arm length and practice the flutter kick: alternating leg movement from the hip, knees slightly bent, feet relaxed and pointed.

Common errors: cycling (bending knees excessively), kicking from the knee only, spreading legs too wide.

Coaching cues: Long legs floppy ankles, Kick from your hip not your knee, Leave bubbles behind you not beside you.

Progression: (1) Practice at wall -> (2) One arm on kickboard -> (3) Both arms on kickboard -> (4) Kickboard vertical (more resistance) -> (5) No kickboard (arms alongside body).

variations.easier: Child holds the wall with both hands, kicking at the surface.
variations.harder: Flutter kick with face in water and rhythmic breathing.

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: Children experiment with different ways of entering the water: jumping with arms up, spinning, touching a target before submerging. Combines entry skills with body awareness.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can jump into the water safely
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut sauter dans l'eau en sécurité

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP creative and controlled water entry
tasks.success_criteria: The child performs at least 3 different entry styles and can describe what they did differently each time.
tasks.setup: Pool edge or low diving platform. Deep pool (minimum 1.5 m). No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Individual performance with group observation. One child at a time.

description_full.en:
Children are invited to invent their own entry styles, beyond a standard jump. Teacher prompts: What if you spin before you enter? Can you touch something before going in? What shape can your body make in the air?

Entry styles: (1) Standard jump with arms up, (2) Spin entry (180 turn), (3) Touch-the-wall-then-jump, (4) Two-foot take-off into tuck, (5) Pencil entry (arms along body), (6) Star entry.

Children can score each other on creativity and safety.

Progression: (1) Single entries -> (2) Combine two elements -> (3) Create a named signature entry.

variations.easier: Children enter from a seated position on the edge.
variations.harder: Design a 3-element entry sequence and teach it to a partner.

author.organization: Collectif Grande Évasion / Université de Lille

description.en: The teacher signals a specific movement through a code (colour, number, shape). Children must decode the signal and perform the corresponding aquatic action immediately.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can move through water independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut se déplacer dans l'eau seul

tasks.goal: I REACT quickly to a water safety signal
tasks.success_criteria: The child correctly decodes and executes the signalled action within 3 seconds.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. Teacher stands at poolside with signal cards.
tasks.group_management: Full group. All children in the water treading water or swimming. Teacher signals; all respond simultaneously.

description_full.en:
The teacher establishes a code system: each colour card or hand signal maps to a specific aquatic action.

Example code: Red = submerge, Blue = float on back, Green = swim 5 m crawl, Yellow = tread water, White = exit the water immediately.

The teacher flashes a signal; children must decode and execute within 3 seconds. This builds reactive decision-making critical for real water safety.

Progression: (1) 3-code system -> (2) 5-code system -> (3) Sequential codes -> (4) Children become the code-master.

variations.easier: Only 2 codes initially - submerge or float on back.
variations.harder: Codes announced verbally in a foreign language.

description.en: An advanced version of Human Submarine: children now propel themselves underwater using only leg kick, hands on sides, focusing on streamlined body position.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim underwater
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager sous l'eau

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP streamlined underwater propulsion
tasks.success_criteria: The child travels at least 5 m underwater using only flutter kick with arms alongside body.
tasks.setup: Pool (any depth). Lane markers at 5 m and 10 m. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Individual in lanes. Pairs observe and give feedback.

description_full.en:
Building on Human Submarine 1, this task removes arm propulsion entirely. The child dives and glides underwater using only flutter kick, arms pressed along the sides of the body or extended overhead in torpedo position.

Key focus: body alignment - no bending at the hips, head neutral, legs providing the only propulsion.

Coaching cues: Be an arrow underwater, Your body is one straight line, Slow kicks not fast splashing kicks.

Progression: (1) Push off wall in torpedo position -> (2) Add slow flutter kick -> (3) Reach the 5 m mark before surfacing -> (4) Underwater obstacle course using only kicks.

variations.easier: Child keeps arms extended forward rather than alongside body.
variations.harder: Child counts underwater by tapping fingers on thigh to build breath control awareness.

description.en: Objects (treasure) are scattered around the pool. Children must dive in, collect as many as possible, and exit the pool with their loot. Team or individual competition.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can jump in and climb out alone
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut sauter et sortir seul de l'eau

tasks.goal: I CAN jump into the water and exit the water in any area of the pool.
tasks.success_criteria: Students enter the pool without any fear of jumping into the water, and quickly exit without difficulty from any point in thepool, without help.
tasks.setup:
The exercise will be carried out in deep water pools, or similar. The equipment required for the exercise are floating objects, namely pullbuoys, and boards.
Between 20 and 25 objects.
tasks.group_management: Participants will be organized into groups of 3 or 5.

description_full.en: Children must be able to balance themselves in the water in an upright position and move around in the water.

variations.easier: Objects float at the surface - no diving required. Focus on entry/exit only.
variations.harder: Child must exit without using the ladder.

tasks.progression: The game will start with one of the students playing as a shark and the rest as treasure hunters. The hunters start the game under the teacher's command, outside the pool, and must try to recover the objects that are floating in the pool. To do this, they must jump into the pool, grab one of the objects and get out again without being caught by the shark. To avoid this happening, the hunters must either be outside the pool or sitting on the edge of the pool. If one of thestudents is caught, he or she must switch places with the shark. The game ends when all the objects have beencollected or the game time has run out. There is no need to repeat the game.
tasks.author_text: Susana Soares, Beatriz Pinho Dias, Bruno Souto Ávila, Elodie Nolwenn Ferreira,Filipe Manuel da Silva Moura, Guilherme Filipe da Silva Teixeira

description.en: In pairs, one child acts as the 'hunter' and must tag the 'prey' only while both are underwater. Develops breath control and underwater coordination.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can hold breath and swim underwater
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut retenir son souffle et nager sous l'eau

tasks.goal: I CAN breathe out (blow bubbles) underwater.
tasks.success_criteria:
The students exhale all the air while they are underwater.
The students identify the sound stimulus (metal hitting the stairs).
The students must react quickly to the sound stimulus to grab the object first.
tasks.setup: Swimming pool, one lane, a kickboard, and an object.
tasks.group_management: Base exercise: Students in pairs with similar skill levels, surface and submerge at the teacher’s signal, given from outside the pool. At the signal, react quickly to grab the object before the partner.

description_full.en: Control the vertical balance in shallow water or the vertical flotation in deep water.

variations.easier: Hunter can tag prey at the surface too - just not when prey is underwater.
variations.harder: Prey carries a small object - hunter must take the object, not just tag.

tasks.progression:
Students are grouped in pairs. They submerge at the teacher’s command, aiming to react quickly to a sound stimulus given by the teacher, to grab an object placed on top of a kickboard faster than their partner. The student grabbing the object first earns 1 point.
 
Variation: The object is non-floating. Students must retrieve it from the bottom of the pool and place it on top of the kickboard.
tasks.author_text: Susana Soares, António Rodrigues da Costa Prenda, Duarte André Caldeira Valente, Francisco Sequeira Torrinha Vidal Fernandes, João Rafael Magalhães Monteiro, Pedro Miguel Pinheiro da Silva

description.en: A navigation challenge: children follow an underwater course marked by cones or ropes, exploring orientation while submerged.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim underwater with confidence
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager sous l'eau avec aisance

tasks.goal: I NAVIGATE underwater using spatial orientation
tasks.success_criteria: The child completes the underwater course in sequence, touching each marker in order without surfacing between markers.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. Underwater course marked by cones, rings, or lane rope segments at various depths.
tasks.group_management: Individual timed runs. Maximum 2 children in course zone at once.

description_full.en:
A course is laid out underwater: markers at varying depths, creating a 3-dimensional navigation challenge. Children study the course map, then swim it underwater, touching each marker in the correct sequence.

Key challenge: spatial memory and 3D orientation underwater.

Scoring: time from dive to final marker touch. Bonus: completing without surfacing.

Progression: (1) 3-marker linear course at 1 m depth -> (2) 5-marker zigzag -> (3) Depth variations -> (4) Course changed between runs.

variations.easier: All markers at the same depth, arranged in a straight line.
variations.harder: Markers are moved between runs.

description.en: Individual version of treasure hunt: the child dives in and collects objects from the pool floor in the shortest time possible. Focus on efficient entry and underwater navigation.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can dive and pick up objects from the bottom
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut plonger et ramasser des objets au fond

tasks.goal: I CAN move underwater maintaining a hydrodynamic position.
tasks.group_management:
Three lanes will be used simultaneously, with one student on each starting block.
At the professor’s signal, who will be standing at the side of the pool, all three students will jump into the water at the same time.
To ensure everyone’s safety, before starting the exercise, all students will be asked to perform a headfirst jump from the side of the pool.
Students who are unable to perform this jump will complete the exercise from the edge of the pool.
It is expected that most students will be able to safely jump from the starting block.

description_full.en:
Able to perform a headfirst jump
Able to open eyes underwater
Able to move underwater

variations.easier: Ring placed at arm reach from the wall.
variations.harder: Two rings placed 3 m apart - collect both in a single dive.

tasks.progression:
Three lanes will be used simultaneously, with one student on each starting block.\r\n
At the professor’s signal, who will be standing at the side of the pool, all three students will jump into the water at the same time.\r\n
 \r\n
To ensure the safety of all students, before starting the exercise, we will ask everyone to perform a headfirst jump from the side of the pool.
Students who are unable to perform this jump will complete the exercise from the edge of the pool.
We expect that most students will be able to safely jump from the starting block.\r\n
tasks.author_text: Susana Soares, Miguel Soares Botelho, Pedro Miguel Moreira Gonçalves, Pedro Miguel Marques de Oliveira

author.organization: ALFAC Team France / Université de Lille

description.en: Children blow bubbles underwater, trying to produce continuous streams or specific bubble patterns. Partners observe and judge. Develops voluntary exhalation.
description.fr: Les enfants soufflent des bulles sous l'eau, en essayant de produire des flux continus ou des motifs spécifiques. Les partenaires observent et évaluent.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can put their face in water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut mettre le visage dans l'eau

tasks.goal: I CAN control my breathing.
tasks.success_criteria:
Be able to exhale completely and thus reach the bottom of the pool
When breathing resumes, inhalation is intense and the mouth is wide open.
tasks.setup: In shallow or mid-depth water. For students with high body density, perform the task with a pull buoy stuck to their chest. For students with low body density (overweight, obese), offer a weighted object to help them sink to the bottom.
tasks.group_management: Work in pairs along the edge. A third student ensures safety. The teacher starts the exercise with a signal.

description_full.en: Students are able to hold their head underwater while holding their breath for at least 15 seconds.D

variations.easier: Begin with face just at the waterline - only lips submerged.
variations.harder: Child tries to exhale in rhythm with a partner.

tasks.progression:
The exercise is carried out according to rules that evolve as progress is made. At the signal, students take a breath and float in the ball position. At the second signal, students exhale and are not allowed to move any part of their body. Rule 1: Touch the bottom only while exhaling. Rule 2: The first of the two to touch the bottom wins.Rule 3: The last of the two to touch the bottom wins (it is forbidden to stop blowing).

Key teaching points to support student learning:
·       Breathe through your mouth and cheeks.
·       Adjust your exhalation according to the rules of the game.
·       Allow yourself to feel your lungs emptying.
·       Become the smallest ball possible..
tasks.author_text: Legrand Antoine, Mekkaoui Léa, Quinmeuld Thomas & Potdevin François, ALFAC team, France

description.en: A waterproof UNO-style card game where each card demands a specific aquatic skill to perform. Wild cards can change the required skill. Develops multiple motor competencies in a playful context.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim and dive independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager et plonger seul

tasks.goal: I CAN perform the swimming tasks shown on the cards
tasks.success_criteria: A child is able to independently perform the swimming task shown on a card and then complete another card until there are no more cards left.
tasks.setup:
The task should be completed in an area where the children are able to perform the required tasks. This requires a suitable depth of water (swimming pool). Depending on their ability level, the cards can be placed face down on the floor, so that the children first have to dive down to pick one up and then solve the task depicted on it.
UNO Sports cards are required.
The space should be large enough for the children to perform the propulsion tasks (approx. 10 metres) and for several children to work side by side. Some children need more space with the rotation
tasks.group_management:
The task can be performed alone, in pairs or in a group.
The cards are shuffled and placed face down on the edge of the pool. The children then need enough space to complete the tasks

description_full.en: The children should be able to swim short distances on their belly and on their back. They must be able to master the swimming basics of breathing and diving. The basics of floating, treading water and rotating are also required. However, these can also be trained further through the task.

variations.easier: Only 2 action types. Colour-coded only.
variations.harder: Actions are combined on cards.

tasks.progression:
For this task, a set of UNO sports cards is placed face down on the edge of a pool. Blue, green, red and yellow cards are required. These colours are shown on a laminated sheet with the cards. The swimming task is explained below. Instead of the UNO number in the centre of the card, there is a picture which indicates the swimming task.
The children then turn over a card. Depending on which card they have drawn, they must complete the corresponding task. They then draw a new card. The game ends when all the cards have been turned over.

Variation: The cards can also be placed at the bottom of the pool. The children then have to dive down and pick up a card. They place it on the edge of the pool and complete the required task
tasks.author_text: Nele Schlapkohl & Sarah Schmidt, Team GER

description.en: Children imitate aquatic animals (fish, jellyfish, crab, shark) using their body in the water. Teacher or peer gives the animal name; children must find the right movement. Encourages body creativity and submersion.

family_prerequisites.en: Child is comfortable in water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant est à l'aise dans l'eau

tasks.goal: I EXPRESS creativity and body awareness through animal movement in water
tasks.success_criteria: The child can convincingly imitate at least 4 different aquatic animals using their body in the water.
tasks.setup: Pool (shallow or deep). No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Full group or small groups. Teacher or child calls the animal name.

description_full.en:
The teacher calls out an aquatic animal. All children attempt to move like that animal.

Animal repertoire: Jellyfish (drift, arms wide, pulsing), Crab (sideways on pool floor), Shark (fast, sleek, fin arm extended), Seahorse (upright, slow kicks), Starfish (spread-eagle float on back), Dolphin (dolphin kick), Frog (breaststroke kick), Snail (slow), Octopus (multiple arm directions).

Progression: (1) Teacher calls -> (2) Child leader calls -> (3) Blind: children draw animal card, group guesses -> (4) Animal chain.

variations.easier: Only 3 animals, practiced one at a time with teacher demonstration.
variations.harder: Children invent a NEW aquatic animal and teach the movement to the group.

description.en: Children throw a floating object and must retrieve it by treading water across the pool. Adds challenge: throwing accuracy determines difficulty of retrieval. Develops treading endurance.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float upright in deep water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter à la verticale en eau profonde

tasks.goal:
I CAN keep myself above the water surface through controlled treading water and perform a   precise throwing movement at the same time.
I KNOW how to use my leg and arm movements to generate enough buoyant force to keep myself above the water surface.
I FEEL safe and physically competent in the water because I can use my strength and coordination in a controlled way.
tasks.success_criteria: The minimum goal is to remain above the water with control, throw accurately, hold the ring steady, and move with coordination
tasks.setup: The equipment needed for this activity includes water polo balls or similar floating balls, floating gymnastics hoops, a laminated sheet, and a waterproof pen. The task takes place in a swimming pool in deep water. Ideally, the game should take place in a 25-metre lane, or even better, across two lanes. Alternatively, it can be carried out in open water, preferably in a freshwater lake during an excursion.
tasks.group_management: The children play in groups of three or more, with these groups often including both boys and girls. Each group should organise themselves so that each player rotates through the roles of throwing, holding the hoop and observing or recording. Roles should switch every minute. Use the laminated sheet to document throws and ensure that everyone is included fairly. Allow around two minutes for recording and rotation after each round.

description_full.en:
Basis Aquatic Skills: treading water 
Movement Knowledge: how to use arm and leg movements to stay in position, generate stability or throwing power, and perform a controlled throwing movement in the water Social Skills: cooperating in pairs or groups, taking turns fairly and giving feedback on successful throws.

variations.easier: Object is placed by teacher - child just swims to retrieve it.
variations.harder: Two objects thrown simultaneously - child must retrieve both before touching the wall.

tasks.progression:
Basic instructions: Form a group of three or more people. One person holds a gymnastics hoop above the water while treading water. The other two then take turns trying to throw a floating ball through the hoop while keeping themselves afloat. After about one minute, swap roles so that everyone has the opportunity to both throw and hold the hoop.

Advanced instruction: Reduce the size of the hoop to make the target smaller, or increase the throwing distance.

Optional instruction:
 Compete against another group! Which team can make the most accurate throws while switching roles fairly and staying coordinated in the water?
tasks.author_text: Nils Strassmann, Nele Schlapkohl & Sarah Schmidt, Team GER

description.en: One child is 'the Shark' at the centre. Others must cross from one side to the other. Anyone touched by the shark while at the surface must submerge. Last one standing wins.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim and go underwater
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager et passer sous l'eau

tasks.goal: I CAN treading water and immerge my-self to adapt to a wave
tasks.success_criteria:
The student can stay stationary on or below the surface to avoid danger when breaking waves pass. 
- Breathing: inhalation is very brief. They can hold their breath for around ten seconds. 
- Remaining stationary: the student can quickly change the direction of their support.
- Remaining stationary: the student can quickly change the direction of their support. Downwards to stay afloat (gather information and see the waves coming). Then upwards to submerge (to let the wave pass over the surface). Finally, they go downwards again to return to the surface..
tasks.setup:
A section of a swimming lane in a pool can be used to set up this situation. A minimum depth of 2m is required.
Equipment needed: at least one board per student participating in generating the breaking wave (two per student if possible: one to sit on for stability in the water and one to generate the wave).
tasks.group_management:
A group of four students is needed to generate a breaking wave. These students must be skilled enough to be able to apply sufficient force in a straight line (from back to front) and coordinate with the other three students to generate a breaking wave.
The instructor, positioned at the edge of the pool, asks the four students to line up two meters in front of the participant.
The instructor gives the signal to the four students to activate their boards at the same time.  
Once the student is submerged, the instructor gives the signal for the student to come back up (e.g., by making a metallic noise underwater).  
The instructor stands ready to intervene (safety guaranteed with the aid of a pole) if the student swallows water or panics..

description_full.en: Treading water for 10 seconds. Immerse yourself for 10 seconds.

variations.easier: Shallow pool - fish are safe when they crouch with chin in water.
variations.harder: Fish must touch three different walls during their crossing.

tasks.progression:
This scenario can be chosen at random and/or occur unexpectedly once the swimmer has begun their course. The element of surprise can be a source of motivation for the student who is swimming and will have to take on this type of challenge. For the other students in the class, the focus on a fun challenge (creating the biggest wave possible) is very important. 
The students generating the breaking wave must hold their boards vertically and move them forward powerfully and in coordination with the others to generate a breaking wave. Sitting on a board allows them to be more stable and effective.
.
tasks.author_text: Albertini Cyril, AEEPS-EPIC, Ministery of Education , FRANCE

description.en: The group creates waves by walking in a circle; one child must swim across the pool against the current. Rotating roles.
description.fr: Le groupe crée des vagues en marchant en cercle ; un enfant doit nager en travers contre le courant.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim across the pool
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut traverser le bassin à la nage

tasks.goal: I CAN adapt  in a rough water
tasks.success_criteria:
The student is able to swim, preferably using the crawl stroke, adapting to three points (listed here in order of priority):\r\n
- Breathing: inhaling is done in opposition to the arrival of the waves (to avoid swallowing water).\r\n
- body tone: the student is able to maintain axial tone, which allows them to stay aligned and move efficiently (avoiding being thrown off course by water turbulence)\r\n
- motor movements: the student adopts a more relaxed arm stroke, which prevents them from hitting the wave crests with their hands and causing additional fatigue.  .
tasks.setup:
An ideal place for this exercise is a swimming lane (preferably 25 metres) in a pool. The depth is not important.
The following equipment is required: at least one kickboard per student to generate the waves (two per student if possible: one to sit on for stability in the water and one to generate the waves).
tasks.group_management:
The learner swims alone.
The other students in the group generate the waves. The rest of the class takes on this role. 
The teacher asks the other students to stand in a straight line along the entire length of the course, one metre apart. The skill level of the other students in relation to the learner is irrelevant. Everyone can participate, regardless of their level.
The teacher walks alongside the learner as they swim, ready to intervene if they notice any signs of significant fatigue or panic after swallowing water.

description_full.en: Swim 25 meters

variations.easier: Waves only - no circular current.
variations.harder: Swimmer must keep their head above water using only breaststroke.

tasks.progression:
The scenario “swimming in rough seas” can be chosen at random and/or come as a surprise once the swimmer has started their course. The surprise effect can be a source of motivation for the student who is swimming and will have to take on this type of challenge. For the other students in the class, it is very important that they engage in a fun activity (making as many waves as possible). 
If the group is small and not all students can cover the entire distance, the teacher can ask them to spread out every meter along only one part of the swimmer's route. However, if the students spread out more than 3 meters apart, their actions will not be able to combine properly to generate a real chop.
Students who generate the waves must hold their boards vertically and move them forward/backward very energetically to generate a real wave. Sitting on a board allows them to be more stable and effective.
tasks.author_text: Albertini Cyril, AEEPS-EPIC, Ministery of Education , FRANCE

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: A tag game where children hold hands in a chain. When tagged, the chain must rotate or reorganise to escape. Tests aquatic problem-solving and coordination.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can move around in water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut se déplacer dans l'eau

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP problem-solving and coordination with others in water
tasks.success_criteria: The chain remains unbroken for at least 3 tags, with the group collectively choosing escape strategies.
tasks.setup: Pool (shallow or deep). No equipment required. Marked boundaries.
tasks.group_management: Chain of 3-4 children (holding hands or wrists). One tagger. Rotate roles every 3 minutes.

description_full.en:
Children form a chain by holding hands. One child is the tagger trying to tag the END of the chain. The chain must move together and cannot break.

Key challenge: the chain has momentum - the head can dodge but the tail follows with a delay.

Progression: (1) Chain of 3, shallow water -> (2) Chain of 4, deeper water -> (3) Two taggers -> (4) Chain must also complete one submersion every 30 seconds.

variations.easier: Chain holds pool noodle instead of hands.
variations.harder: Tagger must complete a challenge before tagging - buying the chain time.

description.en: Children navigate blindfolded (or with eyes closed) through a simple aquatic course, guided only by verbal instructions from a partner. Builds spatial awareness and trust.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can move in shallow water comfortably
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut se déplacer à l'aise en eau peu profonde

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP spatial awareness and trust in water
tasks.success_criteria: The blindfolded child can navigate 5 m in water using only verbal guidance from their partner, without touching a wall.
tasks.setup: Shallow pool. Simple course (2-3 markers). Eye masks optional.
tasks.group_management: Pairs. One navigates (eyes closed), one guides (eyes open, voice only). Roles rotate after each crossing.

description_full.en:
The navigator closes their eyes and enters the water. The guide stands on the poolside and gives verbal directions only - no physical contact.

Guide commands: Left, Right, Forward, Stop, Go under, Come up. The navigator must translate verbal cues into body movement in a 3D water environment.

Key learning: trust in water requires trust in a partner.

Progression: (1) Simple straight path -> (2) Two turns -> (3) Guide speaks only in metaphors -> (4) Silent version - guide can only use tapping on water as signal.

variations.easier: Navigator keeps eyes open but must not look at their feet.
variations.harder: Guide is 5 m away and whispers.

description.en: Children blow ping-pong balls across the water surface, trying to reach a target or outsmart a partner. Develops controlled exhalation and breath management.

family_prerequisites.en: No prior experience needed
family_prerequisites.fr: Aucune expérience requise

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP controlled exhalation and breath management
tasks.success_criteria: The child can propel a ping-pong ball across a defined water surface using only breath, reaching a 1 m target.
tasks.setup: Shallow pool or gutter. Ping-pong balls (one per child). Target zones marked by lane rope.
tasks.group_management: Pairs or individual. Each child has their own ball.

description_full.en:
Children place a ping-pong ball on the water surface and blow it toward a target. They discover: strong short puffs move the ball fast but unpredictably, long gentle blows give more control.

Game variations: (1) Blow to the other side first, (2) Hurricane - everyone blows in the same direction, (3) Precision - blow ball into a floating hoop.

Key learning: this is the first step toward water exhalation - the child experiences blowing air in a water context with no fear.

Progression: (1) Free blow exploration -> (2) Target 50 cm -> (3) Target 1 m -> (4) Obstacle course for ball.

variations.easier: Blow the ball while standing on the pool steps.
variations.harder: Child floats face down and blows the ball while submerging face.

description.en: A memory game in the water: objects or cards are placed around the pool. Children must swim to each in a remembered sequence, collecting or tagging them in order.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim forward independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager vers l'avant seul

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP working memory and navigation in water
tasks.success_criteria: The child can recall and swim to at least 4 objects in the correct sequence without prompting.
tasks.setup: Pool (any depth). Coloured rings or numbered cards placed at various pool positions.
tasks.group_management: Individual timed runs. Group watches from poolside. Teacher shows sequence on board for 10 seconds.

description_full.en:
Objects are placed around the pool. The teacher reveals a sequence (e.g. Red, Blue, Yellow, Green) for 10 seconds, then covers it. The child must swim to each object in the correct sequence from memory.

Naming the task Dory's Memory references the fish with memory loss - each child is beating Dory's score.

Progression: (1) 3-object sequence -> (2) 4-object sequence -> (3) Objects moved between runs -> (4) Sequence revealed verbally only -> (5) 6-object sequence with one underwater retrieval.

variations.easier: 2-object sequence, objects clearly visible from the pool edge.
variations.harder: Some objects are partially hidden under a floating mat.

description.en: The instructor states water safety claims (true or false). Children must swim to the 'True' or 'False' side of the pool to vote. Combines aquatic motor skill with safety knowledge.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim to a point in the pool
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager jusqu'à un point du bassin

tasks.goal: I ACQUIRE water safety knowledge through active motor engagement
tasks.success_criteria: The child correctly answers at least 70% of safety claims and can explain two of the correct answers.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. Two ends designated True and False. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Full group in water. Teacher reads each statement from poolside.

description_full.en:
The teacher reads water safety statements - some true, some myths. Examples: You should wait 2 hours after eating before swimming (Myth), Cold water shock can cause involuntary gasping (True), A good swimmer is never at risk of drowning (Myth).

Children swim to the True end or the False end. The teacher then reveals the answer and gives a 30-second explanation.

This combines motor activity with water literacy education.

Progression: (1) 5 statements, simple true/false -> (2) 10 statements with nuanced answers -> (3) Children write their own safety statements.

variations.easier: Statements only involve pool rules - concrete and observable.
variations.harder: Statements involve open-water or rip current scenarios.

description.en: Children link hands or feet in a chain and try to cross the pool together. The chain must stay unbroken. Develops cooperation and synchronised movement.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can move through water independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut se déplacer dans l'eau seul

tasks.goal:
I CAN glide together with others as a ‘Multi-link Water Chain’
I KNOW how to minimize resistance in order glide (together) as far as possible
I FEEL important as a link of a human chain to glide together as far as possible
tasks.success_criteria: The minimum goal is to glide as a multi-link chain.
tasks.setup:
No equipment needed. If available, materials can be used to mark the distance (e.g. cones on the edge or rings on the ground).\r\n
The task works in a swimming pool in deep or shallow water. An edge of the pool is needed for the push-off. The distance needs to be twice as long as the accumulated height of all learners (chain-links).
tasks.group_management: Initially in self-selected pairs or groups of three, later extension by joining two pairs, max. four persons due to the limitation of holding the breath.

description_full.en: Basis Aquatic Skills: fully submerging, freely floating, low-resistance gliding, adjusted breathing I Knowledge: how to minimize resistance in water I Social Skills: cooperating with others in a pair of two or a group of three to four learners

variations.easier: Chain holds a pool noodle between each person.
variations.harder: Chain crosses without the lead swimmer using their arms.

tasks.progression:
Use picture attached for illustration.\r\n
Basic instruction: Pair up with a partner and try to glide as far as possible linked together as a human chain. The linkage should be between the feet of the first person and the hands of the second person. Try both ways of linkage and find out together how to extend the covered distance.\r\n
Advanced instruction: Now find another successful pair and try again with three or four chain links. Try different ways of linkage and find out together how to extend the covered distance.\r\n
Optional instruction: Challenge another pair or team! Who’s the champion of Multi-link Water chain?
tasks.author_text: Inga Fokken & Ilka Staub, Team GER

description.en: Children balance on pool noodles while trying to knock others off. Anyone who falls must submerge before rejoining. Develops balance, resilience and voluntary submersion.

family_prerequisites.en: Child is comfortable being splashed and going underwater
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant est à l'aise avec les éclaboussures

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP balance, resilience and voluntary submersion
tasks.success_criteria: The child can balance on a noodle for 10 seconds and willingly submerges when they fall off.
tasks.setup: Shallow pool (max 1.2 m). Pool noodles (one per child). Wide open area.
tasks.group_management: Full group. Each child has a noodle. Teacher sets time rounds.

description_full.en:
Each child mounts a pool noodle - sitting astride it like a horse, or lying across it belly-down. The goal: stay on as long as possible while others try to knock you off by creating waves.

Critical rule: falling off is part of the game, not a failure. The child must submerge cleanly when they fall, surface, and immediately remount.

Progression: (1) Individual balance exploration -> (2) Wave-making neighbours -> (3) Noodle jousting -> (4) King/Queen of the pool: last one staying on wins.

variations.easier: Child lies belly-down on noodle like a seesaw.
variations.harder: Child must stay on while swimming the noodle across the pool.

description.en: Children move freely in the shallow pool in all directions at varying speeds. At a signal, they must immediately stop and freeze in whatever position they are in. Develops body awareness and spatial orientation.

family_prerequisites.en: No prior experience needed — suitable for beginners
family_prerequisites.fr: Aucune expérience requise — idéal pour débutants

tasks.goal: I BECOME aware of my body and surroundings in water
tasks.success_criteria: The child moves freely for 30 seconds and freezes immediately on signal, holding whatever position they are in.
tasks.setup: Shallow pool. Wide open area. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Full group. Everyone moves freely. Teacher uses a whistle or visible signal to trigger freeze.

description_full.en:
Children move freely in the shallow pool with no instruction on HOW to move. They can walk, hop, swirl, splash, paddle, drag, spin. The only rule: when the teacher signals, everyone FREEZES immediately.

The teacher can narrate the frozen positions: Oh look, we have a frozen swimmer! And a frozen frog!

Progression: (1) Free movement + freeze -> (2) Teacher names a movement before the freeze -> (3) Children call the movement -> (4) Themed freeze poses.

variations.easier: Freeze in the shallows - children can stand still without balance challenge.
variations.harder: Freeze must include at least one arm or leg not touching the pool floor.

description.en: Pairs clap hands above and below water while treading water, synchronising rhythm and movement. Builds coordination, peer interaction and confidence in deep water.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float upright in deep water
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter à la verticale en eau profonde

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP coordination and social connection in deep water
tasks.success_criteria: The pair can maintain synchronised clapping for 10 consecutive beats while treading water in deep water.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Pairs. Each pair works in their own space.

description_full.en:
In pairs, children tread water facing each other in deep water. They clap hands together - above water on beat 1, below water on beat 2. The rhythm alternates: clap high, clap low.

This requires both children to maintain their own upright position while reaching toward each other.

Variants: (1) One child submerges and claps from below while partner reaches from above, (2) Three-way clapping, (3) Speed clapping.

Progression: (1) Slow rhythm -> (2) Tempo increases -> (3) Eyes closed -> (4) Child switches to treading on back.

variations.easier: Begin in shallow water where children can stand.
variations.harder: Trio - three children clap in a circular sequence while all tread water.

description.en: In pairs, children gently splash each other with increasing intensity, then both submerge together on a count of three. Desensitises to water contact and builds peer trust.

family_prerequisites.en: No prior experience needed
family_prerequisites.fr: Aucune expérience requise

tasks.goal: I BECOME comfortable with water contact on face and body
tasks.success_criteria: The child willingly submerges after the mutual splash sequence, without closing eyes in distress.
tasks.setup: Shallow pool. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Pairs. Child pairs chosen by teacher to match comfort levels.

description_full.en:
In pairs, children face each other in the shallow pool. They begin by splashing water ONTO each other gently - legs, arms, shoulders. Intensity increases step by step (teacher calls levels 1-5). At the teacher signal, both children count 1-2-3 together and both submerge simultaneously.

The simultaneous submersion is key: neither child is done to - both do it together at the same moment.

Progression: (1) Gentle arm splash only -> (2) Face splash (if willing) -> (3) 3-count submersion -> (4) Who can submerge first? -> (5) Simultaneous underwater handshake.

variations.easier: Self-splashing only - child splashes themselves to desensitise.
variations.harder: Partner submerges first and waits underwater while the other counts to 3 and joins.

description.en: Children jump into the pool trying to make the biggest splash possible. Points for height and splash size. Builds confidence in entry and water impact.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can jump into the pool
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut sauter dans la piscine

tasks.goal: I ENTER the water confidently aiming for maximum splash
tasks.success_criteria: The child jumps in with full commitment, creating a visible splash, and surfaces calmly.
tasks.setup: Deep pool (minimum 1.8 m). Pool edge. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Individual turns. One child at a time. Rest observe and cheer or judge the splash.

description_full.en:
The goal is simple and liberating: jump in and make the BIGGEST SPLASH possible. There is no wrong way to do this.

Judging: the group scores each jump from 1-5. Criteria: size of splash, commitment (no hesitation), style.

Key pedagogical value: reframing water entry from a fear moment to a performance moment. The biggest hesitators often become the biggest splashers.

Entry styles: Cannonball (tuck), Bomb (knees up), Star (arms and legs wide), Pencil (thin entry).

Progression: (1) Any entry -> (2) Named entry style -> (3) Score for splash height -> (4) Team score.

variations.easier: Entry from a seated position - slide off the edge into the water.
variations.harder: Jump and complete a 180-degree turn before entering.

description.en: Children use their bodies to 'write' letters or draw shapes on the water surface in groups. Others must guess what is being drawn. Encourages creative collaboration and body awareness.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float on their back or belly
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter sur le dos ou le ventre

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP body awareness and creative expression in water
tasks.success_criteria: The child can form a recognisable letter or shape using their floating body, identified by at least one partner.
tasks.setup: Pool (any depth). No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Groups of 3-5. One child performs, others guess. Rotate.

description_full.en:
Children use their bodies to draw letters, numbers, or shapes on the water surface - floating on their back, spread-eagle, curved, or in combination.

Solo graffiti: one child floats to form a letter (curved body = C, straight arms = T). Group graffiti: two or three children connect bodies to form larger letters.

Variant: Living word - a group spells a short word using their bodies in sequence.

Progression: (1) Individual vowels -> (2) Consonants -> (3) Two-person combinations -> (4) Group spells a water-safety word (SWIM, SAFE, HELP).

variations.easier: Child forms a simple shape (circle = curl up, star = spread wide) rather than letters.
variations.harder: Children must form letters while submerged.

description.en: Children jump in one after another at different angles and positions, imitating a 'crazy fish'. Partner scores the creativity. Builds entry confidence in a playful, low-pressure setting.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can jump safely into the pool
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut sauter en sécurité dans la piscine

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP creative water entry and peer feedback
tasks.success_criteria: The child performs at least 2 distinct creative jumps and provides specific feedback to a partner.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. Pool edge. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Pairs. Partner 1 jumps, Partner 2 scores. Switch roles after 3 jumps each.

description_full.en:
Children jump in imitating a Crazy Fish - imagining what a fish that has gone completely mad might look like flying through the air into the water.

Partner feedback: the observing partner gives two pieces of feedback - one thing they liked, one suggestion.

Key learning: creative performance in water normalises playful risk-taking.

Progression: (1) Any crazy entry -> (2) Partner requests a specific animal -> (3) Scored performance -> (4) Pair jump - both enter simultaneously.

variations.easier: From seated position - slide in sideways, backwards, or in a curl.
variations.harder: Both partners jump simultaneously from different positions and must land without colliding.

description.en: Children form a line holding feet of the one in front, creating a 'fish train'. The train must navigate the pool without breaking. Leadership rotates to the front of the train.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float and kick forward
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter et se propulser vers l'avant

tasks.goal: I COOPERATE with others in a coordinated aquatic movement sequence
tasks.success_criteria: The train of 5+ children crosses the pool without breaking, with clear rotation of leadership.
tasks.setup: Pool (shallow or deep). No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Groups of 5-8. Line formation, each child holds the ankles of the child in front. Teacher signals leadership rotation.

description_full.en:
Children form a line - each holds the feet/ankles of the person in front. The front child (the engine) powers the group across the pool. On the teacher signal, the engine releases, swims quickly to the back, and becomes the last carriage.

The constant rotation means every child takes a leadership turn and every child takes a passive turn.

Progression: (1) Train of 3 -> (2) Train of 5 -> (3) Add one submersion per length -> (4) Train must navigate around a central obstacle -> (5) Two trains race.

variations.easier: Each child holds a pool noodle horizontally, and the child behind holds the other end.
variations.harder: Train travels backwards - engine swims on their back.

description.en: Classic tag variant in water: one shark chases all fish. Tagged fish become sharks. Last fish standing wins. Played with specific aquatic rules (only swimming, no walking, etc.).

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager seul

tasks.goal: I FEEL motivated
tasks.success_criteria: The student expresses joy and commitment during the exercise.
tasks.setup:
A shallow or deep water pool
Does not require equipment.
The teacher is out of the water.\r\n
 \r\n
tasks.group_management: Groups of 4 students, each group occupying a lane. In each lane, one student is next to the wall, and others are in the middle of the pool, separated from each other.

description_full.en: Move autonomously in the water.

variations.easier: Fish are safe when touching the pool wall.
variations.harder: Fish must swim a 5 m underwater stretch to pass through the shark gate.

tasks.progression:
One student is the “shark” and stands near the pool wall, facing it. The other students are the “fish” and move around in the middle of the pool, each separated from the others.
The shark says: “One, two, three, here goes the shark”. While the shark is saying this phrase, the fish must stop moving. When the shark finishes, they immediately turn to face the fish.
If the shark sees any fish moving, that fish must return to the starting position in the middle of the pool.
The first student to touch the wall with their hand becomes the new shark. The student who is the shark when the exercise time ends is the winner.
tasks.author_text: Susana Soares,Maria Madalena Alves de Figueiredo, Manuel Afonso Volta Pinto Moreira, Mauro Luis Pereira Oliveira, Xavier André Alves Ferreira

description.en: Teams compete to collect a ball at the bottom of the pool and bring it to their zone. Combines propulsion, submersion and team strategy.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim and dive independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager et plonger seul

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP teamwork and multi-skill aquatic performance under competition
tasks.success_criteria: The team successfully retrieves the ball from depth and brings it to their zone at least once.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. One heavy ball placed in the centre at depth. Two clearly marked team zones.
tasks.group_management: Two teams of 3-5. Teacher is referee.

description_full.en:
A ball is placed at the bottom of the pool in the centre. Both teams enter simultaneously on the signal. The first player to retrieve the ball brings it to their team zone to score.

Complexity: the ball can be held, passed (above water only), and stolen if dropped. Underwater, no stealing.

Key skills combined: diving to the bottom, swimming with an object, team positioning, passing.

Progression: (1) Ball at 1 m depth -> (2) Ball at 2 m depth -> (3) Teams must pass the ball twice before scoring -> (4) Two balls simultaneously.

variations.easier: Ball is positively buoyant (floating) - no diving required.
variations.harder: Ball must be carried to the zone by the player who retrieved it.

author.organization: ALFAC Team France / Université de Lille

description.en: One child is the 'orchestra leader' who submerges and makes gestures. Others must imitate the gestures from above water, developing risk perception through observation of underwater behaviour.
description.fr: Un enfant est le 'chef d'orchestre' qui plonge et fait des gestes. Les autres doivent les imiter depuis la surface.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can go underwater
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut passer sous l'eau

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP risk perception through underwater communication observation
tasks.success_criteria: The observing children can identify at least 80% of the Conductor gestures from above the water.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: 1 Conductor, group of 4-6 observers. Observers float at surface. Conductor submerges.

description_full.en:
One child is the Conductor. They submerge and perform a series of gestures - musical conducting movements (1-2-3-4 beat, circular arm sweep, point to imaginary section). Observers float at the surface and watch from above, then imitate when the Conductor surfaces.

Key risk perception element: observers must watch carefully to see signs that the Conductor is comfortable (controlled movements) vs stressed (rapid movements, body tension).

Teacher debrief: How did you know the Conductor was OK underwater? What signs told you?

Progression: (1) Conductor holds gesture for 3 seconds -> (2) Gesture sequence of 3 -> (3) Conductor includes a distress signal that observers must identify -> (4) Children learn what real drowning looks like.

variations.easier: Conductor keeps head at waterline, gestures at surface level.
variations.harder: Conductor gestures from 1.5 m depth.

description.en: One child acts as a lifeguard and must 'rescue' other children using a noodle. Children must learn to call for help and accept assistance — developing risk awareness and responsibility.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager seul

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP water rescue awareness and responsible behaviour
tasks.success_criteria: The child acting as lifeguard can reach and support a distressed swimmer using a noodle within 15 seconds.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. Pool noodles. Defined rescue zone.
tasks.group_management: 1 lifeguard per round. 2-3 swimmers who act distressed on signal. Rest observe from poolside.

description_full.en:
One child is the Lifeguard holding a pool noodle. Other children swim normally. On a secret signal, one swimmer acts distressed - calling for help, raising arm, splashing.

The lifeguard must: (1) identify the distressed swimmer, (2) swim to them with the noodle, (3) extend the noodle (not their hand), (4) guide them back to the pool edge.

Critical rule: the lifeguard must NEVER let go of the noodle.

Teacher debrief: Why do we use the noodle instead of our hand? What do you shout to get help?

Progression: (1) Distress is obvious -> (2) Subtle distress signal -> (3) Lifeguard must also shout for adult help -> (4) Two swimmers distressed simultaneously.

variations.easier: Lifeguard stands on poolside and extends noodle - no swimming required.
variations.harder: Lifeguard must reach the swimmer, support them AND return to the pool edge before 30 seconds.

author.organization: University of Health and Sport Sciences Wroclaw

description.en: Children experiment with staying afloat while others create waves. They discover strategies for coping with unexpected water movement — key risk-coping competency.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float independently
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter seul

tasks.goal:
I CAN safely swim in non-standard water conditions (waves).
I KNOW how to behave when swimming in rough water.
I FEEL confident to recognize if I can manage to pass through the waving water.
tasks.success_criteria: Overcome a section of strongly waving water on the surface, without swimming underwater (there are no waves under the water).
tasks.setup:
Shallow water, swimming pool, open water.
Swimming boards that are large enough to create waves for all the participants.
tasks.group_management:
The task can be performed in small or larger groups in lanes or freely.
Instructor outside or inside the pool depending on participants' swimming skills.\r\n
Safety guaranteed by lifeguard.\r\n
For safety reasons, aggression when generating waves should be eliminated before the trial.

description_full.en: Basic swimming skills

variations.easier: Child holds a pool noodle for support while experiencing the waves.
variations.harder: Wave rider must reach a target 5 m away through the wave zone.

tasks.progression: The child's task is to find the easiest/fastest/least tiring way to swim over the section of strongly waving water. Other children stand opposite each other at the bottom of the pool, on both sides of the line (6-8 children separated by a distance of 5-7 m). The children hold boards in their hands with which, when given a signal, they make the waves. At the end of the test, the child who made the waves replaces the practicing child and will be the next to do the test. The task can be done in pairs
tasks.author_text: Team POL, Marek, Anna & Daria

description.en: Objects (jellyfish) float on the surface. Children must swim from one side to the other without touching them — only underwater paths are safe. Develops risk perception and underwater propulsion.
description.fr: Des objets (méduses) flottent en surface. Les enfants doivent traverser sans les toucher — seuls les chemins sous l'eau sont sûrs.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can swim underwater
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut nager sous l'eau

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP risk perception and underwater propulsion
tasks.success_criteria: The child swims from one wall to the other using only underwater paths, without touching any floating object.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. Floating mats, noodles, or balls placed densely on the surface (jellyfish obstacles).
tasks.group_management: Individual or pairs. One child crosses at a time. Others observe and count stings (touches).

description_full.en:
Floating objects (jellyfish) cover much of the water surface. Children must cross from one wall to the other using ONLY underwater paths.

Real risk awareness element: the surface is dangerous. Safe passage requires going beneath the surface.

Scoring: count jellyfish touches. Zero touches = perfect crossing.

Strategic element: children must observe the jellyfish pattern before diving, plan their route, then execute.

Progression: (1) Sparse coverage -> (2) Dense coverage -> (3) Jellyfish moved between rounds -> (4) Crossing must be completed in one breath.

variations.easier: Gaps are left in the coverage - child surfaces briefly in safe zones.
variations.harder: Child must collect a ring from the pool floor while navigating the obstacle.

description.en: Children form letters of the alphabet using their bodies floating or swimming in the pool. Others guess the letter. Builds body awareness and perception of position in water.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float on their back or belly
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter sur le dos ou le ventre

tasks.goal: I DEVELOP body perception and floating awareness
tasks.success_criteria: The child can form a letter recognisable to observers while floating, holding the position for 5 seconds.
tasks.setup: Pool (shallow or deep). No equipment required.
tasks.group_management: Individual performance or group formation. Audience guesses from poolside.

description_full.en:
Children float and use their body position to form letters of the alphabet. Some letters suit floating: A (spread legs), C (curve body sideways), I (straight on back), T (arms wide).

Game structure: teacher calls a letter, children have 10 seconds to form it. Or: children choose their own letter and others guess.

Group letters: two children join floating bodies to form compound letters.

Progression: (1) Easy letters (I, L, T) -> (2) Harder letters (B, R, S) -> (3) Spell a 3-letter word as a group -> (4) Spell a water-safety word in under 30 seconds.

variations.easier: Choose letters from a card - child matches body to a drawn template.
variations.harder: Letters formed while submerged - partner watches from above and guesses.

author.organization: ALFAC Team France / Université de Lille

description.en: Each child attempts to stay underwater as long as possible, alone, exploring their own perception of breathing needs and time under water.
description.fr: Chaque enfant tente de rester le plus longtemps possible sous l'eau seul, explorant sa propre perception des besoins respiratoires.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can go underwater voluntarily
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut passer sous l'eau volontairement

tasks.goal: I EXPLORE my own breathing perception and body awareness underwater
tasks.success_criteria: The child can remain underwater for at least 5 seconds and accurately estimate how long they were under.
tasks.setup: Deep pool. Timer visible from poolside.
tasks.group_management: Individual attempts. Teacher or partner times.

description_full.en:
Each child submerges individually and stays under as long as they are comfortable - there is no minimum target and no pressure. When they surface, the teacher reveals their time.

Key debriefs: How long did you think you were under? (children consistently underestimate). Could you feel your body wanting air?

This task develops interception - awareness of internal body signals.

Progression: (1) Free exploration with no timer -> (2) Self-estimate before time is revealed -> (3) Try to beat previous time by 2 seconds -> (4) Stillness mission - submerge and hold perfectly still.

variations.easier: Child submerges in shallow water where they can immediately touch the bottom.
variations.harder: Child submerges and slowly counts finger-taps to track own time.

author.organization: ALFAC Team France / Université de Lille

description.en: The teacher demonstrates a floating position; children must replicate it exactly. Gradually increase complexity of positions. Develops body perception and imitation.
description.fr: L'enseignant démontre une position de flottaison ; les enfants doivent la reproduire exactement.

family_prerequisites.en: Child can float briefly
family_prerequisites.fr: L'enfant peut flotter un instant

tasks.goal:
I CAN  combine different positions in the water
I FEEL the position of my arms and legs
tasks.success_criteria: be able to reproduce exactly the combined positions shown by his teacher or his classmate without touching the bottom with the feet.
